coredata.dc:title: Investigating the influence of carbon dots on β-Ca2SiO4:Ce3+ phosphors derived from agro-waste for diverse applications

coredata.dc:description: A series of blue-emitting composite based on 3 wt% carbon dots in β-Ca2SiO4:Ce3+ (3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+) was synthesized through a hydrothermal method, aiming to enhance applications in latent fingerprint (LFP) detection, anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures, and optical thermometry. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed that these phosphors possess a monoclinic crystal structure. Under 356 nm excitation, the Ce3+ doped β-Ca2SiO4 phosphors (β-CSO:Ce3+) display a broad blue emission peak at 431 nm, attributed to the 5d → 4f electric dipole transition of Ce3+, while the inclusion of Carbon dots (CDs) shifts this emission to 442 nm. This shift and the improved photoluminescence (PL) intensity are thought to result from the Fӧrster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism. The optimal concentration of Ce3+ ions was determined to be 5 mol%, as higher concentrations led to a decrease in emission intensity due to concentration quenching (CQ). Additionally, a fabricated white light diode using these phosphors achieved chromaticity coordinates of (0.344, 0.330) according to the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE), with the CIE, correlated colour temperature (CCT), and colour purity (CP) metrics indicating a bright green output with values of (0.1486, 0.0433), 1759 K, and 96.4%, respectively. The optimized 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite demonstrated a remarkable CP of 99.9%. Notably, the phosphors maintained 85.77% of their emission intensity at 423 K, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. A novel approach utilizing sketch pen and brush mode was developed to apply the optimized AC security ink, allowing for the creation of various intricate patterns. The resulting AC tags featured high resolution and durability. These findings underscore the 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite as superior luminescent materials for use in fields requiring LFP, AC strategies, and optical thermometry.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A series of blue-emitting composite based on 3 wt% carbon dots in β-Ca2SiO4:Ce3+ (3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+) was synthesized through a hydrothermal method, aiming to enhance applications in latent fingerprint (LFP) detection, anti-counterfeiting (AC) measures, and optical thermometry. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirmed that these phosphors possess a monoclinic crystal structure. Under 356 nm excitation, the Ce3+ doped β-Ca2SiO4 phosphors (β-CSO:Ce3+) display a broad blue emission peak at 431 nm, attributed to the 5d → 4f electric dipole transition of Ce3+, while the inclusion of Carbon dots (CDs) shifts this emission to 442 nm. This shift and the improved photoluminescence (PL) intensity are thought to result from the Fӧrster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) mechanism. The optimal concentration of Ce3+ ions was determined to be 5 mol%, as higher concentrations led to a decrease in emission intensity due to concentration quenching (CQ). Additionally, a fabricated white light diode using these phosphors achieved chromaticity coordinates of (0.344, 0.330) according to the Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE), with the CIE, correlated colour temperature (CCT), and colour purity (CP) metrics indicating a bright green output with values of (0.1486, 0.0433), 1759 K, and 96.4%, respectively. The optimized 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite demonstrated a remarkable CP of 99.9%. Notably, the phosphors maintained 85.77% of their emission intensity at 423 K, showcasing exceptional thermal stability. A novel approach utilizing sketch pen and brush mode was developed to apply the optimized AC security ink, allowing for the creation of various intricate patterns. The resulting AC tags featured high resolution and durability. These findings underscore the 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite as superior luminescent materials for use in fields requiring LFP, AC strategies, and optical thermometry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Eggshell (ES) waste ranks among the top 10 by-products generated by the food processing industry. The disposal of eggshells poses significant challenges, particularly due to the difficulties associated with landfill usage. Consequently, it becomes economically advantageous to convert this waste biomaterial into commercially valuable materials. Natural sources of calcium carbonate (CaCO3) include not only eggshells from chickens and ducks but also shells from marine organisms, birds, mollusks, and snails. Calcium oxide (CaO) serves a critical role in various industrial applications, such as a catalyst in heterogeneous base-catalyzed reactions, a CO2 absorbent, and a dietary calcium supplement. The large-scale consumption of eggs in various industries results in a considerable amount of eggshell waste, which is regarded as an environmental burden. Over time, the accumulation of this ES biomaterial can pose significant environmental risks if not managed correctly [1,2]. ES waste, which is composed of approximately 96% CaCO3, has shown promise as a bio ceramic material. Through processes such as pyrolysis or calcination at temperatures above 900 °C, CaO can be derived from ES waste, presenting a sustainable solution to this environmental issue.

In recent years, environmentalists globally have focused on the agricultural sector to devise effective strategies for minimizing agricultural waste. There are numerous strategies for the sustainable management and repurposing of agricultural waste. Among these, nanotechnology stands out as a highly promising field with significant potential for addressing the issue of agricultural waste. It offers innovative solutions for transforming agricultural by-products into reusable raw materials. These materials can be further processed for various uses, including the synthesis of nanomaterials, showcasing nanotechnology's role in facilitating the long-term conversion of agricultural waste into recyclable raw materials.

Silica (SiO2), one of the Earth's most prevalent elements, is primarily sourced from sand, quartz, flint, and various other minerals. Wheat, a widely grown cereal crop, generates a significant volume of agricultural waste annually, with estimates ranging between 600 and 900 million tons [3] from straw and husk. In recent years, wheat straw has gained attention as a cost-effective and efficient source of SiO2, thereby enhancing its value significantly. The primary form of SiO2 in plants, known as phytoliths, is polymerized Si(OH)4, which is abundantly found in wheat straw [4]. Research indicates that both wheat straw and husk can act as excellent precursors for SiO2 production, containing 50–55% and 80–90% amorphous SiO2, respectively. This discovery positions wheat straw and husk as valuable resources for SiO2 extraction, offering a sustainable solution to agro-waste management [5–7].

Phosphors, materials known for their photoluminescent properties, find diverse applications across various domains including temperature sensing [8–13], forensic science [14,15], and anti-counterfeiting [16–18] measures. In the modern context, nano thermometers, leveraging their distinctive spectral characteristics and robust chemical and physical stability, are at the forefront of advancements in research, technology, healthcare, and commercial sectors. Inorganic nanoparticles doped with Rare-Earth elements have emerged as the preferred option for temperature measurement across a wide temperature spectrum. Key spectroscopic indicators used in luminescence-based thermometers to gauge temperature changes include the Full width at half maximum (FWHM) and emission lifetime [19–22].

Quantum dots (QDs) serve dual roles as sensitizers and surface enhancers, capable of amplifying photoluminescence (PL) by adjusting the optical band gap of target material particles. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have risen to prominence for their outstanding attributes, including low toxicity, high reliability, and intense luminosity, which have broadened their application spectrum. The integration of CDs into phosphors has notably enhanced their structure and light-emitting efficiency [23–28]. Recent investigations into composites combining CDs with Rare-Earth (RE) doped luminescent materials have shown promising results. Specifically, in nanocomposites like YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+, Tb3+ /CDs, the FRET mechanism from CDs to Eu3+ ions has significantly intensified the photoluminescence output [29].

One of personal unique and enduring traits is the fingerprints (FPs). Even identical twins, who share the same genes, possess distinct ridge and furrow patterns, making FPs a reliable marker for individual identification throughout history. Beyond their role in personal identification [30–34], FPs are also utilized in biological research to study genetic inheritance. This highlights the importance of effective FPs visualization techniques, especially since most FPs, composed primarily of lipids from our skin, are invisible to the naked eye. Consequently, developing methods for the easy imaging of LFPs is crucial. Over the years, various techniques for detecting and visualizing LFPs have been developed. These methods range from soaking or spraying, fuming with cyanoacrylate esters, to powder dusting. Each technique has its advantages and limitations; for example, the fuming method can release harmful gases, posing health risks, while soaking or spraying may not be suitable for porous surfaces. Powder dusting, on the other hand, is considered one of the simplest and most effective methods for LFP visualization [35–39]. This technique involves applying fine powders that adhere to the lipids in LFPs, making them visible to the human eye. However, traditional powders such as titanium dioxide and aluminium nanoparticles often suffer from drawbacks like poor resolution and high background noise. To overcome these issues, recent studies have introduced up-conversion nanoparticles, carbon dots, and fluorescent quantum dots as alternatives to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio in LFP visualization. Despite these advances, challenges such as lengthy development times, high costs, and the potential for emission quenching remain [40–43]. These obstacles underscore the ongoing need for innovation in LFP detection and visualization methods.

In recent years, the proliferation of forgeries across various sectors, including consumer goods, pharmaceuticals, microelectronics, automotive parts, and valuable documents, has posed significant challenges. To combat counterfeiting, a range of techniques have been developed, including QR codes [44], radio frequency identification (RFID) tags with electronic tracking capabilities and plasmonic labels [45–47]. However, these methods are becoming increasingly vulnerable to duplication due to rapid technological advancements. This necessitates ongoing research into innovative AC strategies that are both cost-effective and highly secure to effectively deter fraudulent activities.

Fluorescent materials have recently garnered significant interest for their potential application in AC inks. This includes organic dyes/pigments [48–54], polymer dots [55], rare earth materials [56–60], and semiconductor quantum dots [61–66]. Rare earth (RE) metal complexes, in particular, have been the subject of extensive research and development for use in security inks. Despite their advantages, these luminescent materials face challenges such as low brightness and sensitivity, primarily due to uneven particle distribution. Addressing these issues requires refining the luminescent materials with consistent enhancements and introducing new functionalities to bolster AC measures.

In this study, we have developed a novel, highly stable, and efficient blue-emitting phosphor nanomaterial. To our knowledge, there has been no prior investigation into β-CSO:Ce3+ phosphor combined with carbon dots. We successfully synthesized a unique blue-emitting phosphor, 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite, through a hydrothermal process at a relatively low operating temperature of 900 °C, which is advantageous over the conventional high-temperature solid-state reactions. The optimized composite was evaluated for their potential applications in optical thermometry for temperature sensing, latent fingerprint detection, solid-state lighting, and anti-counterfeiting measures. Our comprehensive analysis covered the phosphors synthesis, structural and morphological, photoluminescence properties, as well as temperature and concentration-dependent quenching effects. Furthermore, we investigated the CIE colour coordinates, CP and CCT of the 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite, providing a holistic understanding of their performance and applicability in various fields.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), oxalic acid (C2H2O4), citric acid (C6H8O7), nitric acid (HNO3), and CeO2 (99.9%) were procured from Sigma Aldrich Co., India. These chemicals were utilized as received, without undergoing any additional purification processes. Wheat husk, used in the experiment, was sourced from a local milling facility in Tumkur, Karnataka, India.

β-Ca2SiO4:Ce3+ phosphors, with cerium concentrations ranging from 1 to 9 mol%, were synthesized through a three-step process using the conventional solid-state reaction method. The specific procedures employed are outlined in detail in the subsequent sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CaO from egg shells
ce:section.ce:para: White ES were initially rinsed under tap water to eliminate any residual albumen and then air dried at ambient temperature for 12 h. Subsequently, the ES were broken down into smaller fragments. These fragments were evenly spread across a rectangular aluminium tray, designed to maximize surface area for optimal air flow. The tray filled with ES pieces was then inserted into a furnace and heated at 900 °C for a duration of 4 h to undergo sintering. This process resulted in the production of white ES powder, which was further pulverized to achieve a fine powder consistency as shown in Fig. 1a .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SiO2 from wheat husk
ce:section.ce:para: Using regular tap water, wheat husk was cleaned to get rid of dust and dirt. Following that, wheat husk was dried for 24 h at room temperature and evenly distributed into a large, rectangular aluminium tray. To provide enough air circulation and a uniform dispersion of wheat husk powder, flat trays have been chosen. After the wheat husk tray was heated to 400 °C for 8 h and then to 800 °C for 10 h to initiate sintering, white wheat husk ash was produced. This was then finely ground to produce a powder ( Fig. 1b)

The following chemical equation was used to produce CSO phosphors doped with Ce3+ ions:
(1)
2CaO +
SiO 2
+
CeO 2
=
Ca 2
SiO 4
Ce +
O 2
↑

The stoichiometric ratio of β-CSO:Ce3+ (1–9 mol%) phosphors is produced utilizing the solid state method, with the use of the aforementioned chemical equation. An agate mortar was used to weigh and grind the stoichiometric amounts of CaO from ES, SiO2 from wheat husk, and CeO2 (99.99%) from Sigma-Aldrich. For 10 h, the ground powders were sintered at 1250 °C ( Fig. 1c).

To begin the synthesis process, 10 mL of distilled water was mixed with 2.0 g of urea and 1.0 g of citric acid, stirring the mixture for 10 min. Subsequently, 0.05 g of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors, pre-dissolved in 3 mL of water, was introduced to the mixture. This mixture was then agitated vigorously for an additional 10 min before being transferred into a 100 mL autoclave to undergo a reaction process for 8 h at a temperature of 200 °C. After cooling, the resultant material was washed with ethanol and subjected to centrifugation at 4500 rpm for three cycles to finalize the synthesis. Notably, the CDs were directly infused onto the surface of β-CSO:5Ce3+, eliminating the need for a dialysis step. The synthesized sample was then dried at 80 °C for 6 h for further analysis and applications. Moreover, the methodology allows for the straightforward and efficient production of large quantities of these composites without necessitating an extensive procedure, as illustrated in Fig. 1d.

Fresh FPs were obtained from a consenting adult volunteer and transferred onto various surfaces, including a ceramic plate, aluminium foil, and glass. The procedure for collecting the donors FPs involved the following steps: first, the donor washed their hands with soap and then gently brushed their fingertips across their nose. This action was done to ensure a light coating of oils on the fingertips, which aids in the transfer of the fingerprint. Subsequently, the donor pressed their fingertips onto each of the mentioned surfaces to deposit their LFPs. The deposited LFPs were then delicately dusted with the specially optimized phosphor powder using a soft feather brush, followed by gentle blowing with a hand blower to remove excess powder. Finally, photographs of the FPs were taken directly on the scene using a 12 M pixel Nikon D3100/AF-S digital camera under the illumination of a 365 nm UV light source, as shown in Fig. 2a .

A solution was prepared by mixing distilled water (83%), NH4OH (1% w/w) to adjust the pH, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) serving as a binder. To this mixture, 3 mg/L of sodium dodecyl sulfonate was added to regulate the inks surface tension. The mixture was then stirred mechanically for 45 min to ensure thorough dissolution. Subsequently, 0.5 g of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphor was incorporated into the solution, stirred continuously for 30 min, and subjected to ultrasonic treatment at 25 kHz for 60 min to achieve a homogeneous dispersion. This prepared ink was then utilized to design AC patterns on various substrates including craft paper, medical cards, plastic covers, painted wood, and wrappers, employing a straightforward sketch pen and brush mode. The designed patterns were documented in situ with photographs taken under both daylight and UV light at 365 nm illumination ( Fig. 2b).

The structural characteristics of the synthesized phosphors were evaluated using a powder X-ray diffractometer (PXRD, Shimadzu 7000) employing CuKα radiation (1.541 Å). The field emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL-JEM-2100) was utilized to investigate the surface morphologies of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM-JEM-2010) is used to measure the particles size and the surface morphology. To assess the elemental binding energies, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed using a Vacuum Generator (VG) ESCALABMKII spectrometer equipped with an enhanced AlKα source. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were conducted with a Jobin Yvon Spectrofluorimeter Fluorolog-3, using a Xenon lamp (450 W) as the light source for excitation. For visual documentation of the LFPs and AC patterns, a Nikon D3100/AF-S digital camera was employed.

Fig. 3a displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of both un-doped and β-CSO:Ce3+ (1–9 mol%) phosphors, along with their respective miller indices (hkl). The observed XRD peaks matches with ICSD: 81096 β-CSO [67], confirming the formation of Ce3+ doped β-CSO phosphors. These phosphors crystallize in a monoclinic structure with unit cell dimensions a = 5.53 Å, b = 6.67 Å, c = 9.13 Å, and a cell volume of 340 Å3. Given that the ionic radius of Ce3+ ions (120.2 pm) is intended to substitute Ca2+ sites (132 pm) in the lattice, this substitution is plausible considering the ionic size difference. Literature suggests that for a dopant ion to successfully substitute a host site without significantly distorting the lattice, the difference in ionic radii (Dr) should not exceed 30%. In this study, the Dr between the Ca2+ site and the Ce3+ ion was calculated using the following formula [68]:
(2)
D r
=

Here, Rd and Rs represent the ionic radii of the Ce3+ ion and the Ca2+ site, respectively. The Dr was calculated to be approximately 9%, demonstrating that Ce3+ ions were successfully incorporated into the Ca2SiO4 lattice in place of Ca2+ ions. A noticeable shift in the peak at 2θ = 32.13° in the XRD pattern indicates the successful doping of Ce3+ ions into the host β-CSO matrix (Fig. 3b).

Fig. 3c shows the XRD of 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ and CDs a hump at 2θ = ∼20° might be attributed to the CDs. Furthermore, the XRD pattern for the synthesized CDs, illustrated in Fig. 3d, exhibit a broad hump around the 2θ value of ∼20°, which is attributed to the (002) plane characteristic of amorphous carbon. It was also observed that the peak intensities became stronger and the FWHM narrowed upon the introduction of CDs, suggesting that the integration of CDs into the matrix prompts redox reactions that enhance the crystallinity of the host material [69]. The XRD patterns of CaO from ES and SiO2 from wheat husk are presented in Figs. 3e & f, respectively. Upon calcination of ES powder at 900 °C for 2 h, the emergence of a cubic phase was observed, consistent with JCPDS No. 82–1690, indicative of CaO formation [70]. Furthermore, the XRD analysis of rice husk ash, calcined at 600 °C for 4 h, displayed a broad peak with maximum intensity at 2θ = 21.6°, suggesting the amorphous nature of SiO2 [71].

The Scherrer's relation offers a straightforward approach to estimating crystallite size by analysing the broadening of diffraction peaks [72]. Utilizing the FWHM of the most prominent peak, this method calculates the average size of the crystallites as per Eq. (3).

In this formula, D represents the crystallite size, k is the shape factor (typically 0.9), λ signifies the wavelength of CuKα radiation (0.154060 nm), βD is the FWHM of the most pronounced peak, and θ is the angle of diffraction. By rearranging eq. (3), the expression for the integral breadth can be derived.
(4)

Lattice strain in powder samples, caused by crystal defects, distortion, and lattice mismatch, can be quantified using the Stokes and Wilson formula [73]:

In this context, ε represents the lattice strain and β represents the integral breadth associated with strain effects. The calculated values for crystallite size (D) and strain are presented in Table 1
.

The Williamson-Hall (WH) plot integrates the effects of size broadening and strain broadening. It employs an accessible integral breadth method that distinguishes between crystallite size-induced and strain-induced line broadening by analysing the peak width as a function of the 2θ angle [74]. The broadening of peaks resulting from lattice strain is described using the Stokes-Wilson formula [75,76].

Upon correcting for instrumental broadening, the observed line width is the sum of the strain-induced broadening (βstrain) and the size-induced broadening (βD).

eqs. (8) and (9) are known as Williamson-Hall (WH) equations, where eq. (8) takes the form of a straight-line equation, y = mx + c. The WH plots for samples were constructed using the 4sinθ values on the x-axis and βhkl cosθ values on the y-axis [77], as demonstrated in Fig. 4a . From the linear regression line, crystallite size was determined from the y-intercept, and strain was assessed from the slope. The values for crystallite size (D WH) and lattice strain (ε WH) are listed in Table 1 .

Furthermore, crystallite size (DHW) and lattice strain (εHW) were estimated using βhkl and planar spacing dhkl (the distance between adjacent planes in the crystal lattice) through Halder-Wagner (HW) analysis [78]. The HW method introduces an alternative approach that involves the integral width, β⁎, of the reciprocal lattice point and the interplanar spacing (d⁎).

Eq. (13) takes the form of the linear equation y = mx + c, where the slope is represented by kλ/D(HW) and the intercept by 16 ℇH-W2. In the Halder-Wagner (HW) approach [79], a plot of y = (β/tanθ) 2 vs x = βcosθ/sin2θ is constructed, as depicted in Fig. 4b. The linear regression applied to the data points enables the calculation of crystallite size and lattice strain from the slope and intercept, respectively, the obtained values documented in Table 1.

The Size-strain plot (SSP) method is employed for analysing crystal structures deemed isotropic [80–85], offering insights into both the size and strain of phosphors. Within SSP analysis, the Lorentzian distribution is utilized to characterize crystallite size, while the Gaussian distribution is applied to assess lattice strain [86,87].

By constructing a plot of (dβcosθ) 2 vs (d2βcosθ) as depicted in Fig. 4c, the crystallite size and apparent lattice strain were determined from the slope and intercept of the extrapolated linear data [88], respectively, and are recorded in Table 1. Regarding Ce3+ ion concentration, Fig. 4d illustrates the average crystallite size of the samples plotted against Ce3+ concentration, demonstrating a reduction in crystallite size growth rate upon the introduction of Ce3+ dopant. The dislocation density (δ) and micro-strain (ε) in the phosphor, influenced by Ce3+ doping, were evaluated using two methodologies [89,90], as outlined in eqs. (16–18).

In this equation, D represents the average crystallite size, θ is the Bragg's angle, β is the FWHM of the most intense peak, and Δdhkl signifies the deviation between the observed (d0) and standard (ds) interplanar spacings. For these assessments, the D values are derived from the Scherrer's eq. A positive micro strain, indicating tensile residual stress, occurs if d0 < ds. Conversely, a negative micro strain, indicating compressive residual stress, is noted if d0 is less than ds. The strain measurements for each plane of the doped samples are systematically compiled in Table 1.

The microscopic properties of the carbon dots were determined using TEM analysis and are shown in Fig. 6a. The results indicate that the particles are spherical, homogeneously monodispersed, and with no form of aggregation. The estimated particle size is ∼3.25 nm, and the size distribution histogram (Fig. 6b) presents a narrow size distribution of the CDs, which also attests to the size of the CDs. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 6a) showed distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm corresponding to the (100) of the lattice planes of CDs and implies high crystallinity of the CDs. Fig. 6c shows the TEM image of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphor clearly reveals flaky and agglomerated particles with size ∼7–10 nm. In addition, the size distribution is used to estimate the average particle size and is found to be 6.49 nm (Fig. 6d). The TEM image of 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite is shown in Fig. 6e and the corresponding average particle size of CDs in composite is found to be 4.7 nm (Fig. 6f). The HR-TEM image of 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite is depicted in Fig. 6g and the average inter-planar distance (d) of the composite material is 0.22 nm, which is located in the (100) plane of CDs. The large number of carboxylic acid components that CDs typically have at their surface gives them a high affinity for water. They are ideal for further integration with other polymeric, organic, inorganic, or biologically active substances. From TEM image of 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite, tight adherence of the individual CDs to the surface of β-CSO:Ce3+ can be seen, which is clearly explained by the binding between carboxylic acid components and β-CSO:Ce3+, thus distinguishing the boundaries between CDs and the surface of β-CSO:Ce3+ could be done. Further, the lattice spacing (d) between lattice planes of phosphor, CDs and phosphor are estimated and found to be is ∼0.22, 0.24 and 0.26 nm respectively ( Fig. 6h-j). In addition, the lattice spacing (d), calculated using GATAN software, revealed values of 0.22, 0.24 and 0.26 nm for (100), (220) and (103) planes, respectively ( Fig. 6k-m). Significantly, the inter-planar d-spacing derived from HR-TEM images closely matches the GATAN software results.

The XPS analysis of β-CSO:5Ce3+ was conducted to investigate its surface elemental composition and oxidation states, as presented in Fig. 7a. The high-resolution XPS spectra for the Ca 2p core levels are shown in Fig. 7b, revealing two peaks, Ca 2p1/2 and Ca 2p3/2, arising from orbital splitting, with an energy gap of 3.38 eV between them, without applying any fitting constraints. The Si 2p spectrum features a doublet of 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 peaks, fitted based on the intensity ratio, where the intensity of the Si 2p1/2 peak (on the higher energy side) is precisely half that of the Si 2p3/2 peak. The Si 2p peak observed at 101.8 eV in ZrSiO4, attributed to SiO and Si-O-Si bonds, contrasts with our observed peaks at lower and higher binding energies (103.02 eV for Si 2p3/2 and 104.50 eV for Si 2p1/2), as detailed in Fig. 7c. The O 1 s core spectrum, showcasing a significant peak at 531.51 eV, is depicted in Fig. 7d. Deconvolution of the Ce 3d core level reveals two main doublet peaks characteristic of Ce3+ ions (3d5/2 and 3d3/2) at binding energies of 906.85 and 882.27 eV, respectively, along with three satellite peaks at 907.8 (Ce3+ 3d3/2) and 886.68 (Ce3+ 3d5/2) [91], fitted with a Gaussian-Lorentzian mix, as shown in Fig. 7e. The C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum, illustrated in Fig. 7f, identifies two principal peaks at 284.80 and 289.53 eV, indicative of C-C/C=C and CO bonds in CDs, respectively [92,93].

Fig. 8a presents the PLE spectrum of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors, showcasing a prominent and broad excitation peak at 339, 356, 365 and 382 nm within the ultraviolet excitation range of 320–450 nm. This peak primarily results from the 4f → 5d transition of Ce3+ ions [94]. The PL emission spectra, depicted in Fig. 8b and recorded within the 380–500 nm region, reveal a strong emission peak at 431 nm, attributed to the 5d → 4f spin-allowed transition of Ce3+ ions. Fig. 8c illustrates how the PL intensity varies with different concentrations of Ce3+ doping in the β-CSO:Ce3+ phosphors. It is evident that the PL intensity peaks at a Ce3+ ion doping level of 5 mol%, beyond which an increase in doping concentration leads to a decline in PL intensity due to concentration quenching. This phenomenon occurs as the Ce3+ ion concentration increases, reducing the distance between Ce3+ ions and enhancing their interactions. Consequently, a concentration quenching effect emerges from the non-radiative energy transfer among Ce3+ ions. To elucidate the energy transfer mechanisms among the dopant ions, the critical distance (Rc) between dopant ions is analyzed as follows [95]:
(19)
R c
= 2
3 V
4 π
X c
N
1 / 3
where Xc represents the concentration at which quenching occurs for the dopant ions, NN denotes the number of host cations that can potentially be replaced, and VV is the volume of the unit cell. By substituting the values N = 4, Xc = 0.05, and V = 450.28 Å3, the calculated Rc value is approximately 16.26 Å, which is significantly larger than 5 Å. This indicates that concentration quenching (CQ) is primarily due to electric multipole-multipole interactions between Ce3+ ions. The nature of these interactions can be identified using Dexter's energy transfer theory and Reisfeld's approximation [96], where Dexter's formula is as follows:
(20)
1 x
= K
1 + β
x
− Q
3

In this formula, I represents the emission intensity, X is the doping concentration, K encompasses several constants, and θ signifies the type of interactions occurring. The values of θ can be 3, 6, 8, or 10, corresponding to exchange interactions, dipole-dipole (d-d), dipole-quadrupole (d-q), and quadrupole-quadrupole (q-q) interactions, respectively. To ascertain θ's value, a plot of log X versus log (I/x) is used, as illustrated in Fig. 8d. From the plot, a slope of −1.32 is derived, which implies a θ value of approximately 3.96, suggesting that exchange interactions are predominant in causing concentration quenching in β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors. The CIE (International Commission on Illumination) chromaticity coordinates for β-CSO:Ce3+ (1–9 mol%) were determined and are depicted in Fig. 8e, indicating a cyan-blue emission. The CCT was calculated using methodologies referenced in prior works. The cyan-blue nature of the emission was further characterized by the CCT, calculated via the Mc-Camy's formula [97];
(21)
CCT = − 437
n 3
+ 3601
n 2
− 6861 n + 5514.31

In this context, n is calculated using the formula n = (x − xc)/(y − yc) and (xc, yc) represents the chromaticity epicentre coordinates, specifically (0.332, 0.186). The average CCT, utilized to quantify the hue of a light source, and was determined to be approximately 1759 K, as depicted in Fig. 8f. Typically, a CCT value above 4500 K is classified as cool, whereas a value below 3200 K is deemed warm. Hence, the synthesized β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors, with a concentration range of 1–9 mol%, serve as an effective warm blue component for white light-emitting diode (LED) applications. Additionally, the colour purity of the phosphors was evaluated using a specific formula to quantify this aspect [98];
(22)

In this formula, (x, y) represent the CIE coordinates of the product, (xi, yi) are the colour coordinates for standard white light, and (xd, yd) indicate the colour coordinates corresponding to the dominant wavelength. The calculated average CP for the synthesized phosphors was approximately 96.40%, showcasing their significant potential for application in white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs). The values for CIE coordinates, CCT, and CP for the β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors across the range of 1–9 mol% are compiled in Table 2 .

CDs were incorporated to amplify the luminescence of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors, resulting in the formation of 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite. The PL excitation spectrum and emission spectra of composite are depicted in Figs. 9a and b, respectively. The PL excitation spectrum of CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ monitored at 442 nm excitation band from 320 to 410 nm. excitation consists of five absorption peaks at 339, 356, 365, 370 and 382 nm peaks. These five peaks are consistent with the five energy levels of the crystals 5d excited states of Ce3+ [99,100]. The peak at 356 nm was more intense than other peaks in the excitation spectrum. The CIE (International Commission on Illumination) coordinates for the 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite, ranging from 1 to 5 wt%, are presented in Fig. 9c. The CCT was calculated to define the colour temperature of the light source, revealing an average CCT of approximately 1712 K, as shown in Fig. 9d. The calculated average colour purity for these nanocomposites was found to be approximately 99.18%, demonstrating their high potential for use in the development of white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs). The values for CIE coordinates, CCT, and CP of the CDs(1–5 wt%)@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite are summarized in Table 2 .

One possible reason for this phenomenon might entail Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) occurring between CDs and Ce3+ ions. Theoretically, FRET requires three fundamental conditions: Firstly, the donor and acceptor molecules must be in close proximity to each other (typically within 1–10 nm). The TEM image ( Fig. 6 ) of the CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite, revealing the CDs tightly adhering to the surface of β-CSO:Ce3+, supports this requirement. Secondly, there should be an overlap between the excitation spectrum of the acceptor (β-CSO:Ce3+) and the emission spectrum of the donor (CDs). The spectral region depicted in Fig. 9e illustrates where this energy transfer occurs. The broad PL spectra of CDs facilitate achieving substantial spectral overlap between the PL of CDs and the PLE of phosphors, promoting efficient energy transfer. The CDs@β-CSO:Ce3+ composite demonstrates a greater spectral overlap, further supporting the potential for FRET. Consequently, the excitation energy can be transferred more effectively from CDs to β-CSO:Ce3+. Additionally, the orientations of the donor and acceptor transition dipoles typically need to be parallel [101]. FRET facilitates energy transfer through the d-d interaction between the transition dipole moments of the donor and acceptor. When the donor (CDs) is excited to the excited state after absorbing light, two possibilities arise: it will either radiatively relax by fluorescence or non-radiatively transfer energy to the acceptor (Ce3+). Describing the mechanism of luminescence enhancement, electrons in Ce3+ transition from the 2F5/2 to different components of the excited 5d energy level when CDs@b-CSO:Ce3+ are excited at a wavelength of 356 nm, while electrons in CDs undergo a transition from the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) to the Lowest Un-occupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) with the assistance of absorbed photon energy ( Fig. 9f). The emission intensity of 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite demonstrates an ∼17.2 folds increase compared to β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors ( Fig. 9g). The change in FWHM values suggests an enhanced crystallinity in 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite, attributed to a reduction in positive and negative imperfections due to CDs inclusion, resulting in an oxidation/reduction effect. Moreover, the 3 wt% CDs@CSO:5Ce3+ composite exhibit a broad excitation spectrum covering 320–410 nm, with a primary peak at 356 nm, aligning with the emission range (380–500 nm) of prevalent LED chips. This characteristic widens the spectrum of potential applications in luminescent materials, offering enhanced performance and versatility [102]. Table 3 presents a comparative overview of enhanced PL in various CDs composite from different studies [103–108].

Emission spectra at various temperatures (300 to 440 K) for β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite are depicted in Fig. 10a & b. The emission spectra exhibit consistent shapes, and no additional shifts or stray peaks are observed at elevated temperatures. The emission intensity decreases with increasing temperature for both β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite. However, under normal LED operating temperatures (T = 423 K), the normalized emission intensity remains at 85.77% and 90.90%, respectively. The contour plot in Fig. 10c & d visually illustrates the relationship between temperature and emission spectra. When examining stability at 423 K, the normalized luminescence intensity of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors is 85.77% (Fig. 10e), which surpasses that of some commercial red light phosphors such as Y2O3:Eu3+ at 82.21% and Sr2Si5N8:Eu2+ at 82.02%, as demonstrated in Fig. 10f. The symbols c and k refer to the Boltzmann constant.

Many researchers proposed that the temperature-enhanced population of non-radiative surface (trap/defect) state channels is responsible for the thermal quenching of CDs emission. An Arrhenius plot of the integrated PL intensities could be used to quantitatively analyse the relationship between non-radiative trapping and temperature using the equation given below [109]:
(23)
I
T
=
I 0
/
1 + A
e
−
E a
/ kT

I(T) is the PL intensity that depends on temperature (T); A is an indeterminate constant; I0 is the PL intensity at the beginning temperature; and k is the Boltzmann constant. The relationship between ln[{I0/I}-1] and 1/(kT) is shown in Fig. 10g. After linear fitting, it can be determined that Ea = 0.275 eV for β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 0.325 eV for 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite respectively. The Ea symbol represents the activation energy for thermal quenching. The resistance of a phosphor against the thermal quenching of luminescence is generally correlated with the activation energy for the thermal quenching process. The resistance increases with increasing activation energy.

An illustration of the configurational coordinate diagram schematic is provided in Fig. 10h in order to clarify the concept of temperature-dependent phosphor emission. The excitation process is shown by the vertical black lines with arrows. The radiative and non-radiative emission processes are shown by the pink and red dotted lines with arrows, respectively. The ground state electrons transferred to the excited state under the excitation of 355 nm light. Most electrons reverted to the ground state over route 1 (M-N-O; M-N is the non-radiatively relaxed process and NO is the radiative process) at room temperature. But when the temperature rises, the majority of electrons absorb activation energy. If the electron energy is at least as high as the Ea value, i.e., the P point, the activators descend to the ground state via route 2 (M–P–L) without emitting radiation. As a result, a great Ea value indicates a huge energy barrier for thermal quenching, making it challenging for electrons to navigate back to the ground state via route 2. As a result, when raising the temperature, there is not a significant loss of activation energy, suggesting strong thermal stability. Consequently, the high Ea (= 0.275 eV) obtained for the β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphor in this work demonstrated its excellent thermal stability. Moreover, temperature-dependent shifts in the intensity of emissions suggest that it could be used in fluorescence intensity thermometry, a facile optical approach for non-invasive temperature assessment. Extensive scans, fast reactions in biological fluorescent tags, precise estimation of surroundings temperature in healthcare facilities, and non-invasive and direct temperature evaluation of an impassable object are all possible with optical thermometry. The high Ea value of 0.325 eV implies that the CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ are highly stable and, thus, can be used for optical thermometry applications.

The emission peaks strength experiences only minor variations with increasing temperature. This high stability under elevated temperatures underscores the phosphor's significant potential in LED applications. Using formula (24), the changes in chromaticity coordinates ΔX and ΔY for β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite were determined [110].

ΔX and ΔY quantify the shifts in chromaticity, where X1, X2, …, Xn and Y1, Y2, …, Yn denote the CIE chromaticity coordinates at various temperatures, and X and Y represent the average chromaticity coordinates. Calculations yield ΔX and ΔY as 4.96 × 10−3 and 1.6 × 10−3, respectively, demonstrating the superior colour stability of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors (Fig. 11a).

For precise measurements in optical thermometry, the sensitivity ratio (Sr) is crucial. This ratio reflects the thermometer's responsiveness to temperature variations, indicating the degree to which the thermometric parameter alters with each Kelvin. The sensitivity ratio (Sr) can be determined using the specified formula below [111]:
(25)

The sensitivity ratio (Sr), indicative of the temperature measurement capability, was evaluated using eq. (25) and presented in Fig. 11b. For β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors, the Sr value is recorded at 3.4 × 10−2% K−1 at 303 K, and for 3wt.%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite, a comparison of these values with other luminescent sensors based on temperature-dependent FWHM is provided in Table 4 [112–115]. Additionally, the decay lifetime of the synthesized nanophosphors was investigated. Decay curves for β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite, excited at 356 nm, are depicted in Figs. 11c & d, respectively. The luminescence decay curves are accurately described by a bi-exponential function, as shown by the following Eq. [116]:

In this context, I represent the emission intensity at time t, A1, A2 as constants, while τ1, τ2 denote the components of decay time within a double exponential model. The appearance of double exponential decay in phosphorescent materials often signifies underlying energy transfer processes. The average lifetime (τavg) of such emissions can be calculated using the Eq. [117]:

The calculated average lifetimes are 3.236 ms for the β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 4.04 ms for the 3wt %CDs β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite, respectively. In Fig. 11e & f, the internal quantum efficiency (IQE) measurements are shown. The IQE values are determined using the following Eq. [118]:
(28)

LS represents the luminescence spectra of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite, η symbolizes the internal quantum efficiency (IQE) value, and ES and ER denote the excitation spectra for the sample containing the phosphor and the reference BaSO4, respectively. The IQE for β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite has been determined to be 70.99% and 85.98%, respectively, under 356 nm excitation. These values surpass those of other phosphors such as LiCa3MgV3O12 doped with 1 mol% Sm3+ (45%) [119], Li2NaBP2O8 doped with 3 mol% Sm3+ (25.3%) [120], and NaSrLa(MoO4)O3 doped with 4 mol% Sm3+ (23.12%) [121]. This performance underlines the potential of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3wt.%CDs β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite for application in white light-emitting diodes (w-LEDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of LFPs using β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the durability of optimized phosphors and composite, a powder dusting method was employed to assess LFPs on various surfaces (Fig. 12a ). Figs. 12b display the fingerprints revealed using the synthesized β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors and 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite on different types of surfaces including porous (such as cardboard), semi-porous and non-porous (like glass and aluminium foil) under UV light at 365 nm. The images highlight that the phosphors enable the clear visualization of fine print details with high sensitivity and contrast, free from background noise. The phosphors preferentially bind to the fingerprint ridges rather than the valleys due to their nanoscale size and enhanced adhesion properties, a fact underscored by the analysis of grayscale pixel intensity within the highlighted areas of the fingerprints ( Fig. 12c), showing significantly higher intensity in the ridge areas compared to the valleys. Moreover, 3D interactive visualizations of the developed fingerprints on all tested surfaces ( Fig. 12d) further confirm the uniform distribution of the phosphorescent phosphors across the fingerprint surfaces [122].

The challenge of detecting aged fingerprints, which can suffer from the loss of moisture, issues with storage, and difficulties in dusting, underscores the need for a phosphor material capable of overcoming these hurdles. As depicted in Fig. 13a, even fingerprints that have been left exposed to ambient conditions for three months exhibit finely resolved details under both UV light at 365 nm and daylight, indicating the method's effectiveness over time. Although fluorescence intensity and selectivity diminish slightly due to the natural degradation of fingerprint residue (such as the evaporation of amino acids and water, along with the loss of chloride ions) [123] and the accumulation of dust, the essential contrast between the ridges and valleys remains distinguishable. Furthermore, latent fingerprints on glass were subjected to rigorous testing, including exposure to a range of temperatures (100–300 °C) and UV radiation for 30 min, with the developed prints subsequently captured and documented in Figs. 13b & c.

For practical forensic analysis, the photostability and adhesion of composite are paramount. To assess these qualities in composite, we examined their behaviour under exposure to various solvents, including water (H2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and acetone (CH3COCH3), aiming to gauge the stability of developed latent fingerprints (LFPs). The resilience of the LFPs against these solvents was documented in Fig. 13d, under both UV-365 nm illumination and daylight. The LFPs generated on a OHP are shown in Fig. 13e stained with commercial FP powders. The comparison results are clearly shown in the diagram, showing that the 3 wt% CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite is able to display all three types of features, but commercial powders are hindered from exhibiting level II and level III features by severe background interference. As illustrated in Figure 13a
1
-e
1 , the phosphors predominantly adhere to the ridges rather than the valleys of fingerprints, attributable to their nanoscopic scale and enhanced stickiness. This adherence pattern is quantitatively supported by grayscale pixel analysis of the fingerprints, showing a pronounced intensity contrast between ridges and valleys.

A detailed examination of fingerprint patterns developed with β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors on an aluminium foil sheet is presented in Fig. 14a. Fingerprint characteristics are typically categorized into three levels for identification purposes. Level I details encompass the general ridge flow and pattern types such as loops, whorls, and arches. Level II details include minutiae points like ridge ending, bifurcation, and dots. Level III details delve into microscopic features, including ridge pore structure and exact ridge edge shapes. The capture of high-quality level II and III details is vital for forensic identification, as these provide a higher degree of individual specificity than level 1 details alone.

Fig. 14a, g showcases an analysis focusing on the imaging resolution of an LFP developed on aluminium foil, highlighting discernible level I patterns (e.g., right loop), level II minutiae (including bifurcations and islands), and level III features (such as scars and sweat pores) visible to the naked eye [124,125]. LFP can provide all three levels of detailed information for each individual FPs. SEM images of FPs developed on glass. Pores come in various sizes and shapes, including round, oval, rectangle, open and closed. However, pore shape is not consistent, as pressure distortions can alter their basic shape. Fig. 14b, h shows the pore shapes of developed FPs. The positions of the pores on the ridges of the developed FPs on aluminium foil and leaf are depicted in Fig. 14c, i. Fig. 14d, j shows the distance between two pores and are determined to be 138 to 326 μm respectively. The pores diameter of produced FP on glass surface are depicted under various experimental circumstances, with estimated values are determined to be 5023 μm2 to 6528 μm2 and is shown in Fig. 14e, k. Angle of the pores of produced FP are depicted and the values are found to be 141.22° to 179.02° (Fig. 14f, l).

Fig. 15
a, b illustrates AC markings created on a variety of surfaces, including craft paper, medical cards, plastic covers, painted wood, and wrappers, using a straightforward pen technique with β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors serving as a luminescent ink. These markings, barely visible in natural light, become distinctly visible under UV 365 nm light, demonstrating the potential of these phosphors as invisible luminescent inks for anti-counterfeiting purposes, such as for secure signatures or personalized security details. The luminescent ink emits a blue colour when illuminated, offering a novel approach for advanced anti-counterfeiting technologies [126,127]. In a similar manner, Fig. 15c, d showcases AC markings made using a simple brush method with 3wt.%CDs β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite phosphor ink on diverse substrates. These markings, also minimally visible under daylight, become clearly visible under UV 365 nm illumination, indicating their suitability as invisible inks for anti-counterfeiting measures.

The durability of sketch pen mode AC markings under UV light is notable; the cyan blue emission of the AC labels remains consistent for up to 120 min, showing no significant change from 0 to 120 min, as depicted in Fig. 16a. The resilience of the patterns, even when applied to a ceramic mould and subjected to temperatures of 100, 200 and 300 °C, is evidenced by only a slight loss in intensity, highlighting the photostability of these luminescent inks, crucial for their practical application, as shown in Fig. 16b. Further, butterfly patterns drawn on glass surfaces and observed under UV 365 nm light ( Fig. 16c) maintain their integrity for up to three months, showcasing the efficiency of the developed luminescent ink with only a minimal reduction in brightness. Even when exposed to various solvents (water (H2O)), diluted HCl, and acetone (CH3COCH3)), the AC images on fabric substrates exhibit remarkable chemical stability, as seen in Fig. 16d, suggesting that β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors inks are promising for document encryption and the creation of anti-counterfeit items due to their robust stability.

Additionally, the unique pH-responsive optical property of β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors enables a fascinating colour-switch capability [128–131]. Patterns printed on various substrates, like painted wood, initially display blue fluorescent designs under UV light. These designs quickly change to cyan in a hydrochloric gas atmosphere, then swiftly revert to blue in an ammonia gas environment, demonstrating reversible fluorescent colour changes. After 25 cycles, the fluorescent colour of these patterns can be quickly and reversibly altered by simply adjusting the environmental pH level, as shown in Fig. 16e [132,133]. This colour-switchable fluorescent behaviour bestows β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors inks with outstanding potential for high-level anti-counterfeiting applications, such as in food packaging, apparel, banknotes, passports, diplomas, and certificates, promising an innovative future for security technologies.

Moreover, the consistent blue glow of AC labels applied with a brush technique under UV-365 nm light effectively conceals encrypted information for up to 120 min, with the fluorescence of the AC labels remaining largely unchanged from 0 to 120 min, as depicted in Fig. 17a . The photostability of these brush-applied luminescent inks is essential for their real-world utility. Therefore, we have selected an optimized ink formulation to assess the durability of these inks. Illustrations of coffee mug designs drawn on art paper and viewed under UV-365 nm light are presented in Fig. 17b. The coffee mug AC labels, preserved for up to four months, affirm the long-term effectiveness of the luminescent ink used. Conversely, the endurance of these patterns drawn on a chalk piece at temperatures ranging from 50 to 200 °C is examined, as shown in Fig. 17c, revealing that there is only a minimal loss in intensity. This demonstrates that the AC labels sustain minimal damage and exhibit only a slight reduction in brightness over time. Compared to the dip pen method, creating intricate designs with the brush technique is found to be easier, more straightforward, and cost-effective.

This study successfully developed blue-emitting phosphors of β-CSO:Ce3+ (1–9 mol%) using a solid-state method and 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite through a hydrothermal approach, utilizing agricultural by-products such as eggshells and wheat husk. The optimal doping concentration of Ce3+ in CSO was found to be 5 mol%. The CQ phenomenon is attributed to d-d interactions. The 3wt%CDs@β-CSO:5Ce3+ composite demonstrated excellent thermal stability, with a residual luminescence intensity of 90.90% at 423 K and an Ea of 0.325 eV compared to β-CSO:5Ce3+ phosphors. The composite exhibit superior temperature sensing capabilities in the range of 303–443 K, achieving maximum relative sensitivity values of 4.09 × 10−2 %K−1 when compare to phosphors (3.4 × 10−2% K−1). The Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CDs and luminescent Ce3+ ions is proposed as the mechanism behind the luminescence enhancement. Additionally, the composite exhibit broad excitation band, peaking at 356 nm, matches well with the primary emission spectrum of LEDs. In addition, IQE and CP for composite is found to be higher (85.98% & 99.80%) compared to phosphors (70.99% & 96.40%). Furthermore, the optimized composite exhibited high sensitivity and storage stability, making it a promising candidate for multimodal applications.
